q: What does a Structured Streaming checkpoint actually store, and which changes to your query force you to delete it and restart from scratch?
a: A checkpoint directory contains four things. (1) Offsets: the source position processed in each micro-batch (Kafka offsets, Auto Loader file paths, Delta table versions) — this lets a restarted query resume without reprocessing data. (2) Commits: a log of which micro-batches reached the sink, enabling exactly-once semantics. (3) State: for every stateful operator (stream-stream joins, aggregations, deduplication, dropDuplicates) Spark persists the operator's state store contents and its state schema. (4) Metadata: the unique query ID and configuration captured at first run. Changes that break checkpoint compatibility and require deleting it: changing the number or type of streaming sources; modifying Kafka topic subscriptions or Auto Loader paths; any change to a stateful operator's schema or grouping keys (e.g. renaming a watermark column, changing aggregation keys, altering stream-stream join equi-join columns); switching state management backends; changing the output sink type. Changes that are generally safe and do NOT require a checkpoint delete: adding/removing filter steps; changing trigger intervals or rate limits; updating UDF logic; adding columns to non-stateful projections; changing static-side schemas in a stream-static join (the static side is not checkpointed). On Databricks Runtime 18.2+, source evolution mode lets you safely reorder/add/remove sources using the .name() API without losing state. When you must delete, store checkpoints under the Delta table path (e.g. <table>/_checkpoints) so they're protected by Delta's retention policy rather than being orphaned.
fj: I can add a new aggregation column to a streaming query mid-run — the checkpoint will just pick up the extra field.
fs: Adding a column to an aggregation changes the state schema. Spark validates state schema at startup and will refuse to run or silently corrupt state if the checkpoint's stored schema doesn't match the new operator. The safe path is always: change the query, delete or redirect the checkpoint, restart from scratch.
